title: Maximus Planudes

infobox.value: Monk, scholar, anthologist, translator, mathematician, grammarian and theologian

([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Μάξιμος Πλανούδης, *Máximos Planoúdēs*; c. 1260 – c. 1305[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher1991-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3)) was a [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") Greek monk, scholar, [anthologist](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology"), translator, [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"), [grammarian](/wiki/Philologist "Philologist") and [theologian](/wiki/Theology "Theology") at [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). Through his translations from Latin into Greek and from Greek into Latin, he brought the Greek East and the Latin West into closer contact with one another. He is now best known as a compiler of the *[Greek Anthology](/wiki/Greek_Anthology "Greek Anthology")*.[[3]](#cite_note-4)
Older sources give 1330; the transliteration varies; the *Oxford Classical Dictionary* (2009) uses *Planudes*.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDouglasCameron2009-2)
["Maximus Planudes (Byzantine scholar and theologian)"](http://www.britannica.com/biography/Maximus-Planudes). Britannica Encyclopedia. 21 July 1998. Retrieved 13 March 2017.
[British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library"). [{{abbr|Add.|British Museum Additional}} MS 19391](http://www.bl.uk/manuscripts/FullDisplay.aspx?ref=Add_MS_19391), f 19v-20.

Dean, Riaz (2022). *The Stone Tower: Ptolemy, the Silk Road, and a 2,000-Year-Old Riddle*. Delhi: Penguin Viking. p. 146. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0670093625](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0670093625 "Special:BookSources/978-0670093625").

[Kai Brodersen](/wiki/Kai_Brodersen "Kai Brodersen"), Christiane Brodersen: *Planudes, Rechenbuch*, griechisch und deutsch. Berlin 2020 (= *Sammlung Tusculum*). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-11-071192-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-11-071192-9 "Special:BookSources/978-3-11-071192-9"), superseding the incomplete edition of C. J. Gerhardt, Halle, 1865.

Daly, L.W. (1946). "The Greek Version of Caesar's Gallic War". *Transactions of the American Philological Association*. **77**: 78–82. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.2307/283446](https://doi.org/10.2307%2F283446). [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [283446](https://www.jstor.org/stable/283446).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Byzantine scholar (c. 1260 – c. 1305)

| Died | 1305 (aged 44–45)   [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") (modern-day [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")) |

**Maximus Planudes** ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Μάξιμος Πλανούδης, *Máximos Planoúdēs*; c. 1260 – c. 1305[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisher1991-1)[[a]](#cite_note-3)) was a [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") Greek monk, scholar, [anthologist](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology"), translator, [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"), [grammarian](/wiki/Philologist "Philologist") and [theologian](/wiki/Theology "Theology") at [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). Through his translations from Latin into Greek and from Greek into Latin, he brought the Greek East and the Latin West into closer contact with one another. He is now best known as a compiler of the *[Greek Anthology](/wiki/Greek_Anthology "Greek Anthology")*.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maximus_Planudes&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Maximus Planudes lived during the reigns of the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") emperors [Michael VIII](/wiki/Michael_VIII_Palaiologos "Michael VIII Palaiologos") and [Andronikos II](/wiki/Andronikos_II_Palaiologos "Andronikos II Palaiologos"). He was born at [Nicomedia](/wiki/Nicomedia "Nicomedia") in [Bithynia](/wiki/Bithynia "Bithynia") in 1260, but the greater part of his life was spent in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), where as a [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk") he devoted himself to study and teaching. On entering the monastery he changed his original name Manuel to Maximus.

Planudes possessed a knowledge of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") remarkable at a time when [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") were regarded with some hostility by the Greeks of the Byzantine Empire. To this accomplishment he probably owed his selection as one of the ambassadors sent by emperor Andronikos II in 1295–96 to remonstrate with the [Venetians](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") for their attack upon the [Genoese](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") settlement in [Galata](/wiki/Galata "Galata") near Constantinople. A more important result was that Planudes, especially by his translations, paved the way for the revival of the study of [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") and literature in western Europe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Add_19391_19-20.png/250px-Add_19391_19-20.png)](/wiki/File:Add_19391_19-20.png)

The early-14th century map of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") from the [Codex Vatopedinus 655](/w/index.php?title=Codex_Vatopedinus_655&action=edit&redlink=1 "Codex Vatopedinus 655 (page does not exist)"),[[4]](#cite_note-5) sometimes associated with Planudes.

He was the author of numerous works, including: a Greek [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") in the form of question and answer, like the *Erotemata* of [Manuel Moschopulus](/wiki/Manuel_Moschopulus "Manuel Moschopulus"), with an appendix on the so-called "[Political verse](/wiki/Political_verse "Political verse")"; a treatise on [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax"); a biography of [Aesop](/wiki/Aesop "Aesop") and a [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") version of the [fables](/wiki/Fable "Fable"); *[scholia](/wiki/Scholia "Scholia")* on certain Greek authors; two [hexameter](/wiki/Hexameter "Hexameter") poems, one a eulogy of [Claudius Ptolemaeus](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemaeus "Claudius Ptolemaeus")— whose *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")* was rediscovered by Planudes,[[5]](#cite_note-6) who translated it into Latin— the other an account of the sudden change of an [ox](/wiki/Ox "Ox") into a [mouse](/wiki/Mouse "Mouse"); a treatise on the method of calculating in use amongst the Indians;[[6]](#cite_note-7) and *scholia* to the first two books of the *Arithmetic* of [Diophantus](/wiki/Diophantus "Diophantus").

His numerous translations from the Latin included [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero")'s *[Somnium Scipionis](/wiki/Dream_of_Scipio "Dream of Scipio")* with the commentary of [Macrobius](/wiki/Macrobius_Ambrosius_Theodosius "Macrobius Ambrosius Theodosius"); [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid")'s *[Heroides](/wiki/Heroides "Heroides")* and *[Metamorphoses](/wiki/Metamorphoses_(poem) "Metamorphoses (poem)")*; [Boethius'](/wiki/Anicius_Manlius_Severinus_Boethius "Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius") *[De consolatione philosophiae](/wiki/De_consolatione_philosophiae "De consolatione philosophiae")*; and [Augustine's](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") *De trinitate*. Traditionally, a translation of Julius Caesar's *De Bello Gallico* has been attributed to Planudes, but this is a much repeated mistake.[[7]](#cite_note-Daly-8)[[8]](#cite_note-Heller-9) These translations were not only useful to Greek speakers but were also widely used in western Europe as textbooks for the study of Greek.

It is, however, for his edition of the *[Greek Anthology](/wiki/Greek_Anthology "Greek Anthology")* that he is best known. This edition, the [Anthology of Planudes](/wiki/Anthology_of_Planudes "Anthology of Planudes") or Planudean Anthology, is shorter than the Heidelberg text (the [Palatine Anthology](/wiki/Palatine_Anthology "Palatine Anthology")), and largely overlaps it, but contains 380 epigrams not present in it, normally published with the others, either as a sixteenth book or as an appendix.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDouglasCameron2009-2)

J. W. Mackail in his book *Select Epigrams from the Greek Anthology*, has this to add of him:[[9]](#cite_note-10)

:   Among his works were translations into Greek of Augustine's *[City of God](/wiki/City_of_God_(book) "City of God (book)")* and Caesar's *Gallic War* [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*]. The restored Greek Empire of the Palaeologi was then fast dropping to pieces. The Genoese colony of Pera usurped the trade of Constantinople and acted as an independent state; and it brings us very near the modern world to remember that Planudes was the contemporary of [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch").

He is recorded as one of the first people to use the word "million".[[10]](#cite_note-Smith-11)

## Geography (Ptolemy)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maximus_Planudes&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography (Ptolemy)")]

According to Berggren & Jones (2000)[[11]](#cite_note-12) and Mittenhuber (2010)[[12]](#cite_note-13) many of the extant manuscripts of [Ptolemy's Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy)#Stemma "Geography (Ptolemy)") can be connected with the activities of Planudes. Within the [stemma](/wiki/Stemmatics "Stemmatics"), manuscript groups UKFN and RVWC both descend from a recension by Planudes; only manuscript X (**[Vat.gr.191](https://digi.vatlib.it/mss/detail/Vat.gr.191)**) is independent.

Regarding Planudes' work in rediscovering the Geography, an [hexameter](/wiki/Hexameter "Hexameter") poem survives titled: "του σοφωτάτου κυρου Μαξίμου μονάχου του Πλανούδου στίχοι ηρωικοί εις τήν [Γεωγραφίαν](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)") [Πτολεμαίου](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") χρόνοις πολλοίς άφανισθεισαν, είτα δέ παρ' αύτοΰ πόνοις πολλοίς εύρεθεΐσαν."[[13]](#cite_note-14) which can be translated as "Heroic verses by the most wise monk Maximos Planudes on the Geography of Ptolemy, which had vanished for many years and then had been discovered by him through many toils."[[14]](#cite_note-15) The summary of the poem by Berggen & Jones (2010) is as follows:

> "What a great wonder, the way that Ptolemy has brought the whole world into view, just like someone making a map showing just a little city. I never saw anything so skillful, colorful, and elegant as this lovely geographia. This work lay hidden for countless years and found no one to bring it to light. But the emperor [Andronikos](/wiki/Andronikos_II_Palaiologos "Andronikos II Palaiologos") exhorted the [bishop of Alexandria](/wiki/Patriarch_of_Alexandria "Patriarch of Alexandria"), who took great troubles that a certain free-spirited friend of the Byzantines should restore a likeness of the picture worthy of a king."
